text.title: LLM Classifier Design Core Rules
text.summary: Six testable rules for cost-minimised LLM classifiers.

rule.statement: Verdict goes through a single forced tool call (tool_choice={"type":"tool","name":"extract"}). No free-text answer.
rule.rationale: Forced tool delivers schema-correct output reliably; free-text needs parser + retry.

rule.statement: Pick the smallest model that meets κ ≥ 0.7 against the calibration holdout. Do not default to the largest.
rule.rationale: 10-100x cost difference between haiku/flash and opus; calibrated κ tells you the true gap.

rule.statement: Items batched per call by numeric id (1..N) inside the user message; verdicts returned as a dict keyed by id.
rule.rationale: Batching amortises system prompt + tool schema across items; numeric ids prevent alignment drift.

rule.statement: System prefix marked as cacheable. Hit rate ≥ 80% across batches; otherwise the cache is misconfigured.
rule.rationale: System prefix is the largest stable component; caching gives ~75% input-cost cut.

rule.statement: Output schema is fixed (enum, JSON object, score). One verdict per item; no additional properties; no nullable answer.
rule.rationale: Strict schema makes downstream consumers parser-free.

rule.statement: Before shipping, run judge-calibration-protocol against ≥30-case holdout. κ ≥ 0.7 to ship.
rule.rationale: Classifier is a judge; uncalibrated judges leak false-pass/false-fail at unknown rates.

rule.statement: Preconditions hold → wire batched classifier per procedure.
